title: Five Reasons Movie Theaters Are ... Back?! Plus, the World of Wong Kar-Wai.
format: Sean and Amanda ask whether 'Godzilla vs. Kong' really brought theaters back, size up a sleepy SAG-to-Oscars race, then bring in L.A. Times critic Justin Chang to walk through Wong Kar-Wai's filmography for the Criterion box set.

segments.heading: Are Movie Theaters Back?
segments.note: Godzilla vs. Kong and the dual-release future

films.title: Godzilla vs. Kong
films.note: 3,000 theaters, $48.5 million domestic over Easter weekend and an A CinemaScore (A+ from under-18s). Sean and Amanda call it deeply, deeply stupid in the best way, a movie whose title tells you exactly what you're getting, and one worth seeing on a big screen even though it played day-and-date on HBO Max. It's the test case for whether dual release is the future, and for whether Sam Esmail was right to champion it.

films.title: Nobody
films.note: Universal's Bob Odenkirk 'Taken'-style action movie, released in theaters and doing fine but not booming, about $12 million. Amanda's example of the middle-tier earner that used to reliably fill a January slot and may not survive in theaters anymore.

segments.heading: The SAG Awards and a Sleepy Oscar Race

films.title: The Trial of the Chicago 7
films.note: Won the SAG ensemble; Aaron Sorkin, beloved by the Academy. Sean flags it as a plausible Green Book-style Best Picture upset, an accessible film that softens a hard chapter of history, even though he and Amanda were only lukewarm on it.

films.title: Nomadland
films.note: The 800-pound gorilla of the season, not even SAG-ensemble nominated because it leans on Frances McDormand and non-professionals. Framed as a sign of where the new Academy's taste is headed: quieter, more artful movies with room to breathe.

films.title: Promising Young Woman
films.note: Carey Mulligan's vehicle. After Viola Davis's surprise SAG win, Amanda thinks Mulligan won't win Best Actress, and is fine with it because she doesn't love this 'iffy' movie.

films.title: Minari
films.note: The counterfactual: a Minari win here would have signaled a more interesting, harder-to-predict counterbalance than the Nomadland-vs.-Chicago 7 axis the race settled into.

segments.heading: The World of Wong Kar-Wai
segments.note: With Justin Chang, on the Criterion set

films.title: As Tears Go By
films.note: The debut, a gangster love story indebted to 'Mean Streets' and still mostly linear. A hit that emboldened him; caught up with late, and Justin loves its cheesy, perfect Sandy Lam cover of 'Take My Breath Away.'

films.title: Days of Being Wild
films.note: The stylistic departure and a flop at the time, hard to see for years. The young-person's film that opens the loose trilogy; the first place Justin heard his own Shanghainese dialect on screen. Amanda was blown away seeing it last.

films.title: Chungking Express
films.note: Justin's favorite Wong and favorite film, period: two genres smashed together, shot on the fly in a few weeks, the gateway drug's gateway drug. Tarantino championed it via his Rolling Thunder label in 1995. Home to the Faye Wong 'Dreams' apartment montage and 'California Dreamin'.'

films.title: Ashes of Time
films.note: The wuxia outlier, famously recut; one of only a few Wong films left out of the Criterion box (alongside its Redux).

films.title: Fallen Angels
films.note: Grew out of a discarded third 'Chungking' story. Sean's pick for where NOT to start, the most cynical and alienating, but the motorcycle finale set to the Flying Pickets' 'Only You' floored him on rewatch.

films.title: Happy Together
films.note: Justin's first Wong, seen as a teenager: the only gay romance, the most emotionally corrosive, an argument movie shot in Buenos Aires with Leslie Cheung and Tony Leung. The Iguazu Falls image and the Caetano Veloso cue that 'Moonlight' later borrowed.

films.title: In the Mood for Love
films.note: The consensus masterpiece and Amanda's entry point via Sofia Coppola. Shot partly by Christopher Doyle and Mark Lee Ping-bing, its Cambodia ending grabbed to hit a Cannes deadline. No. 25 on the 2012 Sight & Sound poll. Yumeji's Theme is the needle drop.

The epically dragged-out spiritual sequel to 'In the Mood for Love,' where Maggie Cheung becomes a shadow and Tony Leung a cad. Justin's favorite performance in all of Wong: Zhang Ziyi reacting to the transactional money scene.

films.title: My Blueberry Nights
films.note: The English-language one and, for Justin, 'the one stinker of the bunch,' Wong repeating gestures in a new context and hitting a dead end. Left out of the Criterion set.

films.title: The Grandmaster
films.note: His kung-fu film, shot by Philippe Le Sourd, with a train-station sequence Sean rates among the best martial-arts scenes ever. Suffered the Harvey Weinstein U.S. recut; the occasion for Justin's 2013 interview and its balcony cigarette.

films: Shang-Chi and the Legend of the Ten Rings

TV: The Falcon and the Winter Soldier

Shang-Chi and the Legend of the Ten Ring.title: Shang-Chi and the Legend of the Ten Rings
